feedback.text: The Digital Fairness Act should also consider what could be of value to our future generations. In particular, cultural values should be preserved. I am referring to digital media, such as series/films/music, in particular games that have had a great influence on a generation. As an example, ‘The Crew’ has played a major role in revealing new technical and creative areas, and this cultural object is now completely unavailable (outside illegal means). This is detrimental not only to cultural editing, but also to the opportunity to teach game development, or to present a detailed and neutral picture of marketing schools and psychology. Moreover, when acquiring such titles, it is never mentioned that it is a licence rather than ‘purchase’, which indicates permanent possession. The shutdown of such services is never further specified and, despite simple and realistic methods, there is no possibility of continuing to use these games on the EoL. These approaches have been very much appreciated by the development of games, and such games are still popular today and are often used as cultural mile sections to portray. Not only multiplayer titles are affected by unfair disconnection despite technical possibilities, but also some single player titles. I would strongly advise me to look at the complaints of the ‘Stop Killing Games’ petition and the examples that are often accompanied by it and not only refer to games, but also consider films and other digital content. What also plays a huge role in modern games is adventitious content, with a target audience that is far below the age of majority. Loot boxes or other elements of games of chance can be mentioned as examples. Obscuring the value of goods by chopping through the conversion of 2x different digital currencies. The deliberate creation of ‘FOMO’, which is not subject to any technical need. A good example of fair design is Helldivers, a bad design is ‘Fortnite’, all of which have these elements and are therefore particularly dangerous for children and adolescents. Payment institutions are also unlikely to have a say on what digital content can or may not be disseminated, which is to be seen as a monopolistic infrastructure in our modern times, such as Visa & Mastercard being able to ban the sale of some legal content on Steam, causing significant harm where no one can compete. As long as content is legal, payment institutions should be obliged to keep away what the individual is doing with his money. Given that the power of payment institutions lies very significantly outside the EU, it is necessary to bring such allegations under control as soon as possible. Fairness starts when buying, and everyone should be able to buy something without restrictions (beyond legal limits, e.g. age). Listen to the possession of the material.
